sourceTitle: Structure your sidebar for more focused work with teamspaces

questions.question: Which of the following statements best describes an **open** teamspace?

options.text: Anyone can join it, and its content can be viewed even by non‑members.
options.explanation: Open teamspaces allow anyone to join with a single click, and the pages are visible to anyone, even if they haven’t joined yet.

options.text: Only invited members can see the teamspace, and a padlock icon appears next to it.
options.explanation: That describes a closed teamspace, not an open one.

options.text: The teamspace is hidden from everyone unless you are explicitly added as a member.
options.explanation: Private teamspaces are hidden; open teamspaces are visible to all.

options.text: You must request access from a team owner before you can view any content.
options.explanation: Requesting access is required for closed teamspaces, not open ones.

questions.question: What is the primary benefit of **favoriting** a page in Notion?

options.text: It pins the page to the top of your sidebar under Favorites for quick access.
options.explanation: Favoriting adds the star icon, and the page appears at the top of the sidebar under the Favorites section.

options.text: It makes the page read‑only for all collaborators.
options.explanation: Favoriting does not change permissions; it only affects your personal navigation.

options.text: It automatically shares the page with everyone in the workspace.
options.explanation: Sharing requires using the Share menu; favoriting does not affect who can see the page.

options.text: It moves the page into a special "Favorites" teamspace.
options.explanation: Favorites are a sidebar section, not a separate teamspace.

questions.question: When should you consider **collapsing** a teamspace toggle in your sidebar?

options.text: When the teamspace contains information you only need occasionally, such as a General company teamspace.
options.explanation: Collapsing less‑frequently used teamspaces (e.g., General) keeps them out of the way while still keeping them accessible.

options.text: Whenever a teamspace has more than ten pages.
options.explanation: The number of pages isn’t the deciding factor; usage frequency matters more.

options.text: If the teamspace is marked as private.
options.explanation: Privacy status does not dictate whether you should collapse it; you may still need quick access to a private teamspace.

options.text: Only when the teamspace is open, because closed teamspaces cannot be collapsed.
options.explanation: Both open and closed teamspaces can be collapsed; the decision is based on relevance, not type.

questions.question: Which of the following is **not** a recommended practice for arranging your sidebar?

options.text: Keep your department’s teamspace at the top with its toggle open.
options.explanation: This is a recommended practice because you likely start your day there.

options.text: Add every page you ever open to Favorites to ensure nothing is missed.
options.explanation: Adding too many pages clutters Favorites and defeats its purpose of quick access to a handful of key pages.

options.text: Collapse rarely used teamspaces and move them lower in the list.
options.explanation: Collapsing and moving low‑frequency teamspaces helps keep the sidebar tidy.

options.text: Use shared pages (e.g., 1:1 notes) for content you need to access from multiple locations.
options.explanation: Shared pages are precisely for that purpose and should be used accordingly.

questions.question: What happens when you edit a **synced block** that exists in multiple teamspaces?

options.text: The change is reflected in every location where that synced block appears.
options.explanation: Synced blocks are linked; editing one updates all copies automatically.

options.text: Only the original block is updated; the copies remain unchanged.
options.explanation: That would defeat the purpose of syncing; all instances stay in sync.

options.text: A version conflict dialog appears, requiring you to choose which copy to keep.
options.explanation: Notion handles syncing automatically without conflict prompts for synced blocks.

options.text: The block becomes read‑only in all other teamspaces until you manually copy the changes.
options.explanation: Synced blocks remain editable everywhere; they are not locked after an edit.

questions.question: Which type of teamspace **cannot be seen** by a user who is not a member?

options.text: Open
options.explanation: Open teamspaces are visible to everyone, even non‑members.

options.text: Closed
options.explanation: Closed teamspaces appear under "More teamspaces" and are visible, though joining requires an invitation.

options.text: Private
options.explanation: Private teamspaces are hidden from anyone who isn’t a member, protecting sensitive information.

options.text: Archived
options.explanation: Archived is a status for projects, not a distinct visibility type; archived teamspaces are still visible under More teamspaces.

questions.question: When using the **search filter** to find a page, which step is essential to limit results to a specific teamspace?

options.text: Click "Show filters" and select the desired teamspace as a filter criterion.
options.explanation: The filter menu lets you choose "teamspace" as a criterion, narrowing results to that teamspace only.

options.text: Manually type the name of the teamspace before your search query.
options.explanation: Typing the teamspace name does not apply a filter; it only influences keyword matching.

options.text: Hover over the teamspace name in the sidebar and select "Search here".
options.explanation: There is no "Search here" hover option in Notion; filtering must be done via the filter menu.

options.text: Use the "Ask AI" feature; it automatically scopes the search to the current teamspace.
options.explanation: Ask AI can search across the workspace but does not automatically limit results to a specific teamspace without explicit filters.

questions.question: Why might you want to **join** a teamspace even if you only need occasional information from it?

options.text: Joining ensures the teamspace appears under "Your teamspaces," making it easier to access frequently used pages.
options.explanation: Being a member moves the teamspace into the "Your teamspaces" section, reducing the steps needed to reach it.

options.text: Only members can view any content inside a teamspace.
options.explanation: Open teamspaces allow non‑members to view content; joining is not required for viewing.

options.text: Joining automatically adds all pages from that teamspace to your Favorites.
options.explanation: Favorites must be added manually; joining does not auto‑favorite pages.

options.text: Members receive email notifications for every change in the teamspace.
options.explanation: Notifications depend on personal settings, not merely on membership.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains the purpose of **shared pages** listed under the "Shared" section in the sidebar?

options.text: They provide a central place for pages that are accessible to multiple people, regardless of the teamspace they reside in.
options.explanation: Shared pages appear under the Shared heading so you can quickly locate content that has been explicitly shared with you.

options.text: They are private notes that only you can see.
options.explanation: Private notes are not listed under Shared; they stay within the teamspace or personal area.

options.text: They are automatically generated by Notion for every new teamspace.
options.explanation: Shared pages are created by users when they use the Share menu, not auto‑generated.

options.text: They replace the need for a Favorites section.
options.explanation: Shared and Favorites serve different purposes; Shared is for collaboration, Favorites for quick personal access.

questions.question: What is the recommended number of pages to keep in your **Favorites** section, and why?

options.text: A handful—so the list stays concise and you can still see the teamspace each favorite belongs to.
options.explanation: Keeping only a few favorites prevents clutter and maintains context by showing the originating teamspace.

options.text: All pages you ever visit, to guarantee nothing is missed.
options.explanation: Too many favorites overwhelm the sidebar and hide the teamspace context.

options.text: Exactly ten, because Notion limits the Favorites section to ten entries.
options.explanation: There is no hard limit of ten; the guidance is about usability, not a technical cap.

options.text: Zero; Favorites are deprecated in favor of pinned blocks.
options.explanation: Favorites are still a supported feature for quick navigation.
